mechanism_narrative: Persistently high unemployment above 8% signals significant economic slack and depressed consumer demand, leading corporations to lower earnings expectations. This outlook drives negative forward returns as investors de-risk.
evidence_refs: fact:Unemployment remained elevated post-2008 crisis, peaking at 10% and still at 9.0% as of Oct 2011.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 posts positive 60-day returns in more than 50% of instances where UNRATE is above 8.0% over a 5-year period.

mechanism_narrative: In a Zero Interest Rate Policy (ZIRP) environment, rising inflation erodes real yields on cash and bonds, increasing the relative attractiveness of equities. Accelerating CPI thus pushes capital into the stock market.
evidence_refs: fact:Fed has held rates near zero since Dec 2008.
failure_condition: A positive 3-month change in CPIAUCSL is followed by negative 20-day S&P 500 returns more than 50% of the time while FEDFUNDS remains below 0.25%.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneous growth in retail sales and housing starts indicates a broad-based, robust recovery in consumer spending and investment. This joint signal of strengthening aggregate demand is a powerful catalyst for positive corporate earnings surprises and equity returns.
candidate_trigger: 3-month change in RSAFS > 0 AND 3-month change in HOUST > 0
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending and housing are key drivers of the US business cycle.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 fails to generate a positive median 60-day return in periods where both proxies show positive 3-month growth.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained acceleration in industrial production reflects improving supply chain health and rising capacity utilization. This leads to higher corporate revenues and margin expansion, driving positive equity returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:Industrial production is recovering from its 2009 trough.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between the 6-month change in INDPRO and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is not statistically positive (p > 0.05) during recovery periods.

mechanism_narrative: The monthly change in nonfarm payrolls is a timely and direct measure of economic momentum. Positive payroll surprises signal stronger-than-expected growth, leading to immediate upward revisions in earnings forecasts and positive short-term equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The PAYEMS release is a major market-moving event each month.
failure_condition: The median 5-day S&P 500 return following a positive 1-month change in PAYEMS is not positive over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: A contraction in real GDP is the hallmark of a recession. This definitive signal of economic decline triggers broad-based earnings downgrades and a flight to safety, causing sustained negative returns in the equity market.
failure_condition: The S&P 500 has a positive average 60-day return in quarters immediately following a reported contraction in real GDP.

mechanism_narrative: An unexpected increase in the federal funds rate signals the beginning of a monetary tightening cycle. This raises the discount rate for future earnings and tightens financial conditions, causing a negative repricing of equities.
evidence_refs: fact:The start of the 1994, 1999, and 2004 tightening cycles were associated with equity market volatility.
failure_condition: The average 20-day S&P 500 return in the month following a >25bps rate hike is positive over three separate tightening cycles.

mechanism_narrative: Decelerating real retail sales growth indicates weakening consumer demand, which is the largest component of the US economy. This slowdown translates directly into lower corporate revenue growth, leading to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending accounts for roughly 70% of US GDP.
failure_condition: The correlation between the 12-month change in RSAFS and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is non-negative over a 10-year rolling window.
